steps.title: Identify the grammatical structures
steps.content:
The left side of the blank—"With a blend of traditional design elements, such as arched Gothic ceilings, and modern ones, such as floor-to-ceiling windows"—is an introductory prepositional phrase.

The right side—"transitional design splits…"—is an independent clause with subject "design" and verb "splits."

steps.title: Apply the rule for introductory phrases
steps.content:
"With a blend of...floor-to-ceiling windows" is a long introductory prepositional phrase, not an independent clause. The main clause is "transitional design splits the difference between old and new." A comma must separate the introductory phrase from the main clause.

A — "windows;": A semicolon requires an independent clause on both sides. The introductory phrase is not an independent clause. Fails.

B — "windows.": A period would make the introductory phrase a standalone sentence fragment — no verb, no complete thought. Fails.

C — "windows—": An em-dash signals a sharp break, parenthetical, or emphasis. Using it here implies the phrase adds an aside or apposition, not that it introduces the main clause. Fails.

D — "windows,": A comma correctly separates the introductory prepositional phrase from the main clause "transitional design splits the difference between old and new." Correct.
